messages.content:
Based on the provided text, *Electromagnetics II* by Steven W. Ellingson is a third-year undergraduate textbook designed for the second semester of an engineering electromagnetics sequence.

### **Summary of Course Topics**

The curriculum covers a broad range of advanced electromagnetic theory and its practical applications:

*   **Magnetostatics & Current Flow:** Examines magnetic force, the Biot-Savart law, and AC current flow, including the concept of skin depth in imperfect conductors (p. 1, 2).
*   **Wave Studies:** Covers wave propagation in general and lossy media, as well as the reflection and transmission of waves at planar boundaries (p. 1, 2).
*   **Transmission Lines & Waveguides:** Explores various transmission line types (parallel wire, microstrip, coaxial) and waveguides (parallel plate and rectangular), including field analysis for these structures (p. 1, 3).
*   **Optical Fiber & Radiation:** Addresses the application of electromagnetics to optical fiber and the principles of radiation and antennas (p. 1, 3, 4).
*   **Reference Materials:** The book concludes with supplementary data, including constitutive parameters for common materials, mathematical formulas, and physical constants (p. 4).

Lesson 2 — Electromagnetics II - Presentation4 slides
Electromagnetics II: Core Principles
  • Advanced study for electrical engineering students.
  • Building on fundamental electromagnetics foundations.

This text introduces *Electromagnetics II* by Steven W. Ellingson, a peer-reviewed open textbook intended for third-year undergraduate electrical engineering students (p. [S.1]).

**Key Topics Covered**

The textbook serves as the second part of a two-semester sequence, covering the following primary subjects:

Magnetostatics and Current Flow: This includes magnetic force, the Biot-Savart law, current flow in imperfect conductors, and the concept of AC skin depth (p. [S.1], [S.2]).

Wave Propagation and Media: The text examines wave propagation in general and lossy media, as well as the reflection and transmission of waves at planar boundaries (p. [S.1], [S.2]).

Transmission Lines and Waveguides: The content addresses the analysis of various structures, including parallel plate, rectangular waveguides, parallel wire lines, microstrip, coaxial transmission lines, and optical fiber (p. [S.1]).

Radiation: Theoretical and practical aspects of radiation and antenna systems are also included (p. [S.1]).

**Structure of the Material**

The curriculum is divided into five primary sections:

1: Preliminary Concepts (p. [S.1])
2: Magnetostatics Redux (p. [S.2])
3: Wave Propagation in General Media (p. [S.2])
4: Current Flow in Imperfect Conductors (p. [S.2])
5: Wave Reflection and Transmission (p. [S.2])

This section outlines the advanced topics in electromagnetism and physics covered in the curriculum, spanning from guided wave structures to fundamental physical constants.

Key topics covered in pages [S.3] and [S.4]:

Guided Wave Systems: The text explores the theory and application of Waveguides (p. [S.3]), Transmission Lines (p. [S.3]), and Optical Fiber (p. [S.3]). 

Radiation and Antennas: The curriculum includes the study of electromagnetic Radiation (p. [S.3]) and the design and function of Antennas (p. [S.4]).

Reference Materials: The provided sections include essential data for coursework, specifically:
- Constitutive Parameters of common materials (p. [S.4]).
- Comprehensive Mathematical Formulas (p. [S.4]).
- A list of fundamental Physical Constants (p. [S.4]).

Magnetism Concept: The text highlights the unique properties of a homogeneously magnetized sphere, noting that its external field is equivalent to that of an ideal point-like dipole, while its internal magnetic field remains constant (p. [S.4]).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: If a transmission line is constructed with lossy materials, how would the 'skin depth' concept—mentioned as a topic in this curriculum—likely influence the design compared to an ideal, lossless line?
options: The skin depth effect would require the designer to consider increased AC resistance and potential signal attenuation at high frequencies.
The skin depth effect proves that current flows uniformly throughout the entire cross-section of the conductor, simplifying the design.
Transmission lines are immune to skin depth effects due to the dielectric insulation used between conductors.
Skin depth is only applicable to waveguides and has no impact on the performance of transmission lines.
questions.explanation: In imperfect conductors, the skin effect restricts AC current flow to the surface, increasing effective resistance and causing frequency-dependent attenuation, which is a critical design consideration for lossy transmission lines.

questions.question: A spherical magnet produces a field outside its volume identical to an ideal point-like dipole. Based on the study of magnetostatics, what does this imply about the total magnetic flux surrounding a closed surface enclosing the magnet?
options: The total magnetic flux is directly proportional to the radius of the sphere.
The total magnetic flux is non-zero because the magnet has internal field lines.
The total magnetic flux is zero, consistent with Gauss's Law for Magnetism.
The total magnetic flux varies depending on the distance from the sphere.
questions.explanation: Regardless of the internal field geometry, Gauss's Law for Magnetism dictates that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero, as there are no magnetic monopoles.

questions.question: When considering the reflection and transmission of waves at a planar boundary, which parameter primarily dictates how the field components redistribute?
options: The physical thickness of the boundary surface.
The relative constitutive parameters (permittivity and permeability) of the two media.
The specific color of the electromagnetic wave.
The starting position of the source within the first medium.
questions.explanation: Reflection and transmission at a planar boundary are defined by the mismatch in constitutive parameters (primarily impedance, which is derived from permittivity and permeability) of the two media.

questions.question: In the comparison between parallel wire and microstrip transmission lines, why is analyzing the electromagnetic fields (as noted in Chapter 7) necessary for a complete understanding?
options: Because the physical dimensions of the wires are irrelevant to signal propagation.
Because simple circuit theory fails to account for geometry-dependent effects like radiation and propagation modes at high frequencies.
Because transmission lines do not behave according to Maxwell's equations.
Because electric fields only exist inside conductors, not between them.
questions.explanation: Field analysis is necessary because transmission line behavior at high frequencies and in specific geometries involves complex electromagnetic field distributions that exceed the limitations of simple lumped-element circuit models.

questions.question: Why is it important for an engineering student to study 'Wave Propagation in General Media' before 'Wave Reflection and Transmission'?
options: Because reflection and transmission are simply special cases where wave propagation encounters a discontinuity in the medium's properties.
Because general media propagation is computationally simpler than reflection analysis.
Because reflection only occurs in a vacuum, not in general media.
Because textbooks traditionally present topics in alphabetical order.
questions.explanation: Understanding how waves exist and travel within a continuous, homogeneous medium is the foundational step required to then analyze what happens when that wave hits a boundary between two different media.

cards.front: What are the three main areas of focus in Electromagnetics II according to the objective?
What is the phenomenon where AC current flow is restricted to the surface of a conductor?
Name three types of transmission lines covered in the text.
cards.back: Parallel wire, microstrip, and coaxial transmission lines.

cards.front: What unique magnetic property does a homogeneously magnetized spherical magnet possess outside its volume?
cards.back: Its magnetic field is identical to that of an ideal point-like dipole.

cards.front: Describe the internal magnetic field of a uniformly magnetized spherical magnet.
cards.back: The field is exactly constant and aligned along the north-south axis.

cards.front: What is the primary law governing magnetic force mentioned in the beginning of the text?
Which specific wave phenomenon is studied at the interface of two different media?
Besides waveguides, what medium is specifically listed for guided wave propagation in this course?
In the context of the course, what are the two main topics regarding electromagnetic energy propagation and reception?
What is intended as the primary textbook for the second semester of this electromagnetics sequence?
cards.back: Electromagnetics, volume 2 by Steven W. Ellingson.

slides.title: Electromagnetics II: Core Principles
bullets: Advanced study for electrical engineering students.
slides.notes: Welcome to our second course in electromagnetics. We will explore advanced wave phenomena and their practical engineering applications.

slides.title: Foundational Electromagnetics
bullets: Wave propagation in general and lossy media (p. 2).
Current flow effects and skin depth analysis (p. 2).
slides.notes: We begin by revisiting magnetostatics and expanding into how waves behave in complex media and near material boundaries.

slides.title: Guided Waves and Transmission Systems
bullets: Analysis of parallel plate and rectangular waveguides (p. 3).
Parallel wire, microstrip, and coaxial transmission lines (p. 3).
slides.notes: Next, we transition from open-space waves to guided structures, focusing on how different geometries facilitate signal transmission.

slides.title: Radiation and Antenna Theory
bullets: Design and analysis of complex antenna systems (p. 4).
Reference materials for formulas and physical constants (p. 4).
slides.notes: Finally, we cover radiation and antenna theory, which allows us to harness these waves for wireless communication.
